real_questions: Can you give color on what are the higher loss rate portions of your portfolio and why do they have higher losses?
Is there any specific pieces of fraud within any channels?
As you grow out your book, what are the chances you'll need to go down the credit spectrum?
Has the enforcement of federal student loan payments affected you?
Any movement in loan modifications recently or over the past few years?
How do you think about high credit limits — some larger banks have gotten in trouble there?
common_pitfalls: Issuers underestimate S&P's attention to off-take partner risk in whole-loan-sale and loans-to-partner programs. If the partner loses interest, the bank is stuck with the credit exposure.
agency_intel: The credit cycle is a particular S&P interest. Expect drill-down on how the cycle reshapes the loss curve by portfolio.
S&P probes off-take partner concentration extensively. Be prepared to speak to partner stickiness, alternative buyers, and the contingency plan if a buyer exits.

NBFI / private-credit lending is a 2026+ cross-agency theme. S&P frames it as not systemic but operationally intensive — probes underwriting quality, counterparty risk assessment, surveillance, collateral understanding, and safeguards against double-pledged collateral.
Where NBFI lending is in the low double digits or mid-teens as a share of total lending, expect S&P to drill into concentration, the partner roster, and collateral controls.
Recent NBFI defaults (per S&P) could portend greater-than-expected losses. Be ready to address the issuer's specific NBFI exposure trajectory and any recent signal events.
